---
title: "LinkML → data-dict"
subtitle: "Generating a tidyverse data-dict.yaml from a LinkML schema"
---

::: {.callout-important}
Built against **data-dict CLI `0.0.1`**, commit
[`c146bae`](https://github.com/tidyverse/data-dict/commit/c146baec42fceb360252d7670663bddb1f6dcfc7)
(installed from `main`; data-dict has no tagged releases yet). Generated files
target data-dict spec `$version: 0.1.0`. The format is early and still evolving,
so results may differ on newer commits.
:::

## The question

[LinkML](https://linkml.io/) is schema-first: you write one model and generate a
various downstream artifacts from it: JSON Schema, SHACL, OWL, Pydantic, SQL DDL, and so on.
The tidyverse [`data-dict`](https://data-dict.tidyverse.org/) project is a
lighter YAML format for documenting and validating a dataset, with a Rust CLI to
check it.

The two formats describe much of the same concepts: tables, columns, types,
constraints, relationships. As data-dict is an emerging specification format,
this example is an experiment to see if we can reliably generate data-dict.yaml
schema definitions from LinkML schema source. Such a generator would certainly be
helpful for data interoperability, and potentially expose more people to data-dict.

## Short answer

Yes. The generator in this repo (`gen_data_dict.py`) reads a LinkML schema and
writes a valid `data-dict.yaml` from it, without minimal data loss. The result
passes all three data-dict validators, and the same dataset validates under both
LinkML and data-dict, each in its own shape.

## The gist of the example

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 90, 'nodeSpacing': 60}}}%%
flowchart TD
    src["<b>otters.linkml.yaml</b><br><i>source of truth (LinkML)</i>"]

    src -->|"<code>linkml lint</code> / <code>linkml validate</code><br>(check the model and a data instance)"| gen{{"<code>gen_data_dict.py</code>"}}

    gen --> dd["<b>data-dict.yaml</b><br><i>generated, portable</i>"]
    gen --> ddtest["<b>data-dict.test.yaml</b><br><i><code>--emit-source</code> (adds source: pointers)</i>"]

    dd -->|"<code>data-dict validate-spec</code>"| green(["✅ all green"])
    ddtest -->|"<code>data-dict validate-meta</code> / <code>-data</code>"| green

    classDef source fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#4285f4,color:#1a1a1a;
    classDef output fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#7cb342,color:#1a1a1a;
    classDef tool fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#fb8c00,color:#1a1a1a;
    classDef done fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#43a047,color:#1a1a1a;

    class src source;
    class dd,ddtest output;
    class gen tool;
    class green done;
```

## TODO

- [ ] Generalize this example beyond the otters schema to cover many prototypical
  LinkML schemas pulled from public sources, exercising the generator against a
  wider range of real-world constructs.

## Where to go next

- [Mapping & Rules](mapping.qmd) covers how each LinkML construct lands in
  data-dict, plus the type rules I had to dig out of the data-dict validator.
- [Validation Walkthrough](walkthrough.qmd) is the actual run, command by
  command.
- [Findings](findings.qmd) is what I took away from it, including the thing I got
  wrong on the first pass.
